one_liner: A 46-year-old man presents to his primary care physician because his wedding ring no longer fits and his shoe size has gone up three sizes over the last few years.

stage2.hpi_extension: Over the past 4-5 years he has noticed enlargement of his hands, feet, and jaw. His coworkers have commented that his facial features look 'coarser' compared to photos from a decade ago. He reports deep headaches, excessive sweating, snoring that now meets criteria for obstructive sleep apnea, and tingling in both hands consistent with carpal tunnel syndrome. He has also noticed his voice has deepened. He has had difficulty reading street signs on his peripheral vision while driving.
stage2.pmh: Hypertension, new diagnosis of type 2 diabetes last year
stage2.physical_exam: General: well-built man with coarse facial features. HEENT: frontal bossing, enlarged nose and lips, prognathism with an increased inter-dental space, macroglossia. Eyes: bitemporal hemianopsia on confrontation visual field testing. Neck: no goiter. Hands: large, thick, 'spade-like' hands with doughy soft tissue; positive Tinel sign bilaterally at the wrists. Cardiovascular: S4 gallop, no murmur. Skin: diaphoretic, oily, with skin tags.

lab_results.result: IGF-1 (somatomedin C) markedly elevated (4x upper limit of normal). Random GH elevated. Prolactin mildly elevated. TSH, free T4, ACTH within normal limits.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Elevated IGF-1 is the best screening test for acromegaly because it reflects integrated GH exposure and avoids the pulsatile variability of random GH levels.

lab_results.result: Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT): after 75 g oral glucose load, GH fails to suppress below 1 ng/mL.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Normally a glucose load suppresses GH. Failure to suppress GH on OGTT is the gold-standard confirmatory test for acromegaly.

lab_results.result: MRI of the sella with gadolinium: a 1.4 cm enhancing pituitary macroadenoma is seen, with upward extension and compression of the optic chiasm.
lab_results.clinical_significance: A pituitary macroadenoma compressing the optic chiasm accounts for both the hormonal excess and the bitemporal hemianopsia. MRI localizes the tumor for transsphenoidal resection.

stage5.final_clue: He undergoes transsphenoidal resection of the pituitary adenoma with improvement in headaches and visual fields. Postoperative IGF-1 remains mildly elevated, and he is started on octreotide (somatostatin analog) to normalize GH secretion.

debrief.classic_presentation: Acromegaly presents insidiously over years in adults with coarsening of facial features, enlargement of the hands and feet (increasing ring/shoe size), frontal bossing, prognathism (jaw protrusion), widened inter-dental spaces, macroglossia, and a deepening voice. Associated features include hypertension, insulin resistance/diabetes, cardiomyopathy, obstructive sleep apnea, carpal tunnel syndrome, skin tags, and arthralgias. Optic chiasm compression from a pituitary macroadenoma causes bitemporal hemianopsia. In children, before closure of the epiphyseal plates, GH excess causes gigantism.
debrief.pathophysiology: Acromegaly is almost always caused by a benign GH-secreting pituitary adenoma arising from somatotrophs of the anterior pituitary. Excess GH stimulates hepatic production of IGF-1 (somatomedin C), which mediates most of the anabolic and growth-promoting effects on bone, soft tissue, and viscera. GH itself also directly antagonizes insulin action, producing hyperglycemia. Cardiovascular complications (cardiomyopathy, hypertension) and an increased risk of colonic polyps and colorectal cancer make untreated acromegaly a source of significant mortality.
debrief.treatment_overview: Transsphenoidal surgical resection of the pituitary adenoma is first-line therapy. Adjunctive medical therapies include octreotide/lanreotide (somatostatin analogs), cabergoline (dopamine agonist), and pegvisomant (a GH receptor antagonist). Stereotactic radiotherapy is reserved for residual or recurrent disease. Remission is assessed by normalization of IGF-1 and suppression of GH on OGTT.
high_yield_pearls: Best initial test = serum IGF-1; confirmatory test = failure of GH to suppress after oral glucose load (OGTT).
Acromegaly in adults = coarse features, enlarging hands/feet, prognathism, macroglossia; in children (before epiphyseal closure) = gigantism.
Pituitary macroadenomas can compress the optic chiasm and cause bitemporal hemianopsia.
Increased risk of colorectal polyps and colon cancer — screening colonoscopy is recommended.
Treatment ladder: transsphenoidal surgery → octreotide/pegvisomant → radiotherapy.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Endocrine chapter, 'Acromegaly' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Endocrine chapter, 'Growth hormone / Somatostatin analogs' section.
